title: How to Document Your Microdosing Journey for Future Reference
body:
How to Document Your Microdosing Journey for Future Reference

So, you’ve started microdosing. Maybe it’s psilocybin, LSD, or even something like Lion’s Mane mushroom capsules (paid link) alongside your regimen. You want to track what’s happening, how you feel, and what’s changing over time, right? But you’re not sure how to keep that record in a way that actually helps you later. Does that land?

Look, microdosing is subtle. It’s not about giant shifts or overnight miracles. It’s about small changes: a little more focus here, a slight mood lift there. You can’t rely on vague memories or “I think I felt better last week.” You’ve got to write it down, day by day, detail by detail. This is your personal data. Your lived experience. And it’s gold.

Why Documenting Your Microdosing Journey Matters

Honestly, most people start microdosing without a plan for tracking. They just “try it out” and hope for the best. That’s a recipe for confusion. How will you know what dose worked? Which days were good or bad? What external factors might have influenced your mood or energy? If you don’t keep notes, you’ll miss patterns that could make your journey safer and more effective.

Imagine this: it’s week four of your microdose cycle. You’re feeling calmer, but the anxiety is still creeping in on some days. You check your journal and see that the days when you took two micrograms of LSD (or the days you skipped) line up with the worse anxiety. Now you can tweak your dose. Or the days when you combined your microdose with meditation. You want to know that, right?

What to Track in Your Microdosing Journal

Here’s the thing: you don’t need to become a full-time researcher or write a novel every day. But some specifics really matter. This list will keep you focused and give you the data you need.

  • Date and time: When did you take your microdose? Morning? Afternoon? Late night?
  • Dose amount: How much did you take? Be precise. If you’re using Lion's Mane mushroom capsules (paid link) or a measured LSD dose, write down the milligrams or micrograms.
  • Substance source: Where did you get it? Homemade, purchased from a dispensary, or a trusted friend?
  • Physical state: How did your body feel before dosing? Tired, restless, calm?
  • Mental state: Mood, stress levels, clarity of thought before and after.
  • Activities: What did you do that day? Work, exercise, socializing, creative tasks.
  • Notable effects: Any shifts in mood, energy, anxiety, perception, or focus.
  • Side effects: Headaches, nausea, irritability, or anything unexpected.
  • Sleep quality: How did you sleep the night before and after your dose?
  • Diet and hydration: What did you eat or drink? Sometimes this changes how microdosing feels.
  • Other substances: Coffee, alcohol, medications, or supplements like a soft therapy blanket (paid link) for relaxation.

These details might seem small, but over weeks, they create a clear picture of what’s working for you and what isn’t.

How to Keep Your Microdosing Journal

Okay, you’ve got the list. Now, how do you actually keep your journal? Paper? Digital? Voice memos? Here’s what I suggest.

  • Choose your method and stick with it. It’s tempting to switch between apps, notebooks, or voice notes. Don’t. Find one way that feels easy and natural. Consistency beats complexity.
  • Daily entries, even short ones, are better than long weekly dumps. You want to capture the moment, not your memory of it weeks later.
  • Use prompts if you need help. A simple template like: Date, Dose, Mood before, Mood after, Notes. You can expand or contract depending on time.
  • Consider apps designed for journaling or mood tracking. Something like Day One, Journey, or even a simple Google Doc can work well. You want it searchable and easy to update.
  • Include photos or other media if you want. Sometimes a picture captures your environment or your physical vibe better than words.

One microdoser I worked with, Sarah, started with pen and paper. But after a month, she switched to a Google Sheet because she wanted to track mood on a scale from 1 to 10. That made it easier to spot trends. She found that on days she combined microdosing with light exercise, her focus and mood were a solid 8 or 9. Without exercise, it hovered around 6 or 7. Right?!

Examples of What to Write

Here’s a sample entry for you to get a feel:

April 10, 8:00 AM - Took 0.1g psilocybin microdose. Felt tired before dosing, mood neutral. By 10 AM, noticed slight uplift in creativity and calm. Worked on writing article for 3 hours, focus was sharp. No side effects. Skipped coffee today. Slept 7 hours last night, felt rested. Ate oatmeal and green tea for breakfast. Overall mood 7/10.

Now compare that to a day with no dose:

April 11, no dose - Morning foggy, stress about deadlines creeping in. Felt distracted and restless all day. Mood 4/10. Had two coffees, which made me jittery. Sleep was poor last night, maybe only 5 hours. Need to watch caffeine intake.

See how clear those notes are? They give you data to compare and adjust your approach.

Why You Should Include External Resources in Your Practice

Reading and learning while you microdose helps you stay grounded. Books like Entangled Life by Merlin Sheldrake (paid link) can open your mind to new ways of thinking about connection, biology, and healing. It’s not fluff. It’s science and story blended in a way that sticks.

Also, don’t sleep on resources like the research on microdosing at NCBI. Reading peer-reviewed studies keeps you informed about safety, possible benefits, and risks. This helps you hold your journey in reality, not fantasy.

How to Use Your Journal to Adjust Your Practice

Truth is, your first few weeks will be trial and error. You’re feeling out your “sweet spot.” Your journal is your compass. When you see a pattern - like better sleep on days you microdose earlier - you can experiment with timing. When you notice mood dips on certain doses, you can scale back.

One guy I know, Mike, found that microdosing on Mondays gave him a huge productivity boost, but by Wednesday he felt drained. So he switched to every other day. He tracked it all meticulously in his journal and said it changed how he managed his workweek.

Don’t guess. Don’t guess. Write it down. Then tweak based on what you see.

Additional Tips for Keeping Track

  • Include a weekly summary - At the end of each week, write a paragraph or two reflecting on changes, challenges, and insights. This helps keep perspective.
  • Be honest with yourself - Don’t sugarcoat effects or skip writing on “bad” days. Those days often hold key lessons.
  • Track external stressors - Big meetings, personal life events, weather changes. They all affect how microdosing feels.
  • Take breaks and note them - Sometimes stepping off microdosing for a week or two gives you clarity. Write about that too.
  • Consider pairing your journal with a calming ritual - Maybe you light a candle or wrap yourself in a soft therapy blanket (paid link) while you write. It creates a safe space for reflection.

What About Sharing Your Journal?

Look, your microdosing journal is personal. It’s your inner experience laid bare. But sharing it with a therapist or trusted friend can add perspective. If you’re working with a guide or healthcare provider, bring your notes. They’ll appreciate the specifics.

If you want to keep things private but still want support, consider online communities or forums where you can share patterns anonymously. Just be cautious about advice from strangers. Always cross-check with reliable sources.

Keep Your Journal Close but Don’t Obsess

Here’s the thing: we want data, but we don’t want to become slaves to numbers or entries. Your journal is a tool, not a taskmaster. If you miss a day or two, it’s okay. Don’t beat yourself up. The goal is clarity and learning, not pressure.

And if you ever feel stuck or overwhelmed, revisit trusted books like The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). It’s a deep dive into how trauma and the body interact, which can shed light on your healing process, microdose or not.

Resources and Next Steps

If you want to explore how microdosing can fit into a broader conscious healing journey, check out How to Prepare for Your First Microdose on The Quiet Medicine. It’s full of practical advice and real-world tips.

For up-to-date research and news on psychedelics, MAPS is a solid go-to: maps.org. Their work is grounded and evidence-based, helping you stay informed about safety and legal issues.

And if you want to connect with me directly or explore more writings on consciousness and healing, swing by kalesh.love. I share reflections, guides, and updates there.

Remember, your microdosing journey is yours. Keep it honest. Keep it simple. Keep it real.

Disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before making changes to your health regimen.
